content.problemStatement:
There are NNN numbers written on a blackboard. The iii-th number is denoted as AiA_iAi​.

The blackboard is said to be good if, for each pair of indices (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) with 1≤i<j≤N1 \leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N, the sum (Ai+Aj)(A_i + A_j)(Ai​+Aj​) is an even number.

The blackboard might not be good initially, so you want to make it good.

To do this, you can perform the following two types of operations:


Erase a 222 from the blackboard, and in its place write two occurrences of 111.

Of course, this can only be done when there's at least one occurrence of 222 on the blackboard.
Erase two occurrences of 111 from the blackboard, and in their place write a single occurrence of 222.

Of course, this can only be done when there are at least two occurrences of 111 on the blackboard.
The above operations can be performed in any order, and each operation can be used however many times you like.

Find the minimum number of operations needed to make the blackboard good.

It can be proved that it's always possible to make the blackboard good.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of values written on the blackboard initially.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of operations needed to make the blackboard good.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The blackboard is initially not good: it has both a 111 and a 222, and we have 1+2=31+2 = 31+2=3 being odd.
Perform a single operation: remove the two occurrences of 111 from the blackboard, and write a 222.
Now, there are three 222's on the blackboard.
Each pair of them has a sum of 444, which is even. So, one operation is enough.
Test case 222: The blackboard is already good.
Test case 333: Perform two operations: in each operation, replace a 222 with two 111's.
In the end, there will be 777 ones on the blackboard, but it'll be good.
It can be proved that this is optimal.
